title: Chapter 10
text: Chapter Seven N ever, ever trust them, Tabitha had told Lara after that horrifying night. If only they’d both taken the advice . Here Lara was again, doing exactly what she knew not to . Ronin waited behind her, and she found his patience infuriating. It would be so much easier to hate him if he’d just push her. If he’d show his true colors and reveal the monster under his fake skin . Could she trust him to keep his word? His exchange with the gearheads had been different from what she’d seen between other bots. He seemed…evasive, almost confrontational. And he claimed Warlord wasn’t involved. Why be honest about meeting with the ruler of Cheyenne only to lie about the nature of their chat ? Ronin’s behavior wasn’t typical of the bots she’d dealt with. It was too human, and that was unsettling Finally, she turned and tipped her head back, looking up into his face. He watched her with that unblinking stare. The corners of his lips were downturned; was that worry in his expression ? “Well?” she asked. A tiny crease formed between his brows. “We going? I’m tired of being wet .” Ronin’s gaze lingered on her. After a while, he turned and walked away from the gate . Lara followed, head bowed with eyes on her feet to keep the rain out of her face. She was soaked to the bone, her sopping clothes chafing her skin. Her boots were waterlogged, squishing with each step. And, to top it all off, the temperature was dropping rapidly with the coming sunset . She opened her pack and stuffed the food inside. Then she wrapped her arms over her chest, tucked her hands into her armpits, and clung to whatever warmth she could muster . When she glanced up to make sure Ronin was still ahead of her, something at the edge of her vision caught her attention. She turned her head, breath fleeing her lungs. Lara had never seen so much greenery. Beyond the road, the ground was blanketed in lush grass, which in turn was bordered by tall, living trees. Their leaves swayed in the wind, shimmering as they turned from one side to the other . She walked to the edge of the field and crouched. Extending her hand, she ran her palm over the grass. It tickled her skin. She pressed down, testing its give, amazed at how soft it was . “I think this is how much of the world used to look,” Ronin said from the street . “Why does it only look like this here ?” “Because bots…maintain. Many of us exist only to repair what is broken. To make it appear whole again .” She plucked a few blades of grass and raised them to her nose, inhaling. “It smells so fresh.” Her hands were red and approaching numbness, but what did that matter, in the face of such wonder ? “Come. We need to get you out of the rain .” Despite everything, Lara was reluctant to move on. She ran her gaze over the seemingly endless green. What would it look like during the day, with the sun shining? If she walked away, would it still be here when she returned ? “You can see it from my residence .” “But not smell it.” She stood up and faced Ronin . “When it’s trimmed, you can .” She wasn’t entirely sure what he meant, but she didn’t bother asking. Warmth. That had to be her focus . They continued along the street, the green landscape remaining to their left as the path curved to the right. Up ahead were more lights, brighter and somehow cleaner than the orangey-yellow ones here. It was a familiar glow — she saw it every night from her little shack on the other side of the wall . Ronin led her around another bend in the road, and suddenly it opened into a wide, long stretch lined with buildings on both sides. Not the pieced-together, rickety little shacks the humans lived in; these were real buildings, made of bricks and wood and concrete, with their glass windows intact and their doors actually attached to their hinges . This was nothing like what she’d seen. Even the buildings in the Market couldn’t compare to the pristine majesty of the Bot District . Had it truly been this way everywhere, before everything fell apart? Even the ruins she normally scavenged? It seemed impossible, but the proof was here, right before her eyes . Still, something was wrong. Everything was too perfect, too clean, too quiet. Things existed here, but nothing lived . The buildings reminded her of the bots. Well-maintained and varied, no two quite the same, but all similar nonetheless . She glanced at Ronin. Maybe not all bots … He lifted an arm and pointed down a street branching to the left. Compared to the main road, it was dark and narrow. The street lamps, spaced further apart, only ran down the right side of the road. On the opposite side, trees stood in silent vigil as far as she could see. Strange that it still seemed inviting. More natural . “Not much farther,” Ronin said . Lara was so lost, gazing at the greenery across the street, that she almost bumped into Ronin when he stopped. She looked around, shaking off her confusion. Up ahead, the street they were on ended, meeting with a road running east to west. Beyond that loomed another portion of the wall . “This way.” Ronin stepped through a gate in a high wall of bushes . She followed him, stopping to stare up at the house on the other side. It was a huge, dark shape, its upper floors — as though the second story weren’t enough, there was a third-floor window wedged under the peak of the roof — bathed in the gentle glow of the nearest lamp. Her shack allowed a couple feet of clearance for her head. How much did this place have ? How much did a person need ? For that matter, how much did a bot need ? Ronin went up the steps to the front door, where he fished something out of his pocket. He fiddled with the handle for a few seconds before swinging the door open. Without a word, he went inside . Lara mounted the steps, freezing at the top. The impenetrable darkness beyond the doorway was not welcoming . “This is it,” he said from within. It sounded like he tossed his pack on the floor. “You coming in ?” “I can’t see .” “You can’t. Just a moment.” His boots thumped across the floor. There was a soft click . Lara turned away from the sudden flare of light, shielding her eyes with her arm. When her vision finally adjusted, she looked inside, mouth agape . The lights on the ceiling filled the place with bright, pure light, putting her little lantern to shame. She entered slowly. This first room was big enough to fit her shack three times over within its pristine white walls. On the left, a set of stairs led to the next level, and straight ahead was an opening into another room. Even the floor seemed to glow; it appeared to be made of wooden boards, but she’d never seen wood so smooth and shiny . Ronin walked past her and closed the door, locking it. Lara went to it after he moved away, tugging on the handle, tapping on its front. Solid and secure . She’d only walked a mile, but had entered an entirely different world . “There are bedrooms upstairs, if you’d like to select one .” “ Bedrooms ?” “Rooms with beds in them.” He gestured to the stairs . Lara moved to their base and stared up. More thick darkness concealed the top. “I just… go up ?” “Do you want me to go first?” Ronin asked. She glanced at him. Was that the hint of a smile on his face ? “Is there something up there I should worry about ?” He reached past her and flicked another switch. Light obliterated the darkness above . “ No .” “Okay.” Lara took a deep breath and set her foot on the first step, cringing at the squish of water from her boot . “You can take them off, if you want .” She didn’t need to be told twice. Tugging them off, she dropped them on the floor beside the first step. Her feet were pale, skin wrinkled, and the floor beneath them was cold. She climbed onto the bottom step and looked down. The material on the stairs was different; soft and dry and cushioned, with little fibers that slipped between her toes . Continuing to the top, she stopped at the landing; the dim hallway ran to either side. She located another of the little switches, and, tentatively, flicked it up. Light filled the hall . There was a door at each end, with two more on the wall opposite the stairs and another to her left. A place like this could house ten or fifteen people, at least. Why would anyone — especially a bot — ever need so much space to themselves ? She turned left, opening the door at the end of the hall, and felt around on the wall until she clicked on the light. This was another large room. A huge bed stood against the far wall, draped in a thick blanket, a decorative wooden panel at its head and foot. The thing was large enough for three adults to sleep in comfortably . She walked to it and ran her hand over the blanket, marveling at the soft material. When she leaned down, the bed sank in slightly, yielding a bit to her weight. It was about as different from her hard pallet as she could have imagined . Another piece of furniture stood near the door. As tall as her chest, it was crafted of dark wood, its face comprised of five wide drawers. She opened them one at a time, finding them all empty . Her curiosity brought her to the swaths of cloth hanging on the wall beside the bed. There was a window behind them, blocked by thin, flimsy plastic strips. Lara bent the strips down to look through the glass, catching a glimpse of the trees and field across the street . She set her bag atop the drawers. Apart from the discomfort caused by her wet clothing, there was another pressing need she’d neglected . “Should be able to get more clothes for you to put in there,” Ronin said . Lara started, looking to the doorway. How long had he been standing there ? “In…here?” Lara slid open the top drawer . “That’s what it’s for. It’s called a dresser .” “Never seen anything as nice as this.” Each of the drawers had a little metal handle, with two on each of the top ones. “What else is supposed to go in it ?” “Just clothes, usually .” “Who has that many clothes?” she asked, bewildered. Most of the clothing she owned was on her now, and it wouldn’t fill half of one drawer. She could think of a thousand other things to store in here . “You put whatever you want in it,” Ronin said. “I’ll go get you something dry to wear.” He turned to leave . “ Wait !” He stopped and looked at her . “I need to go,” Lara said . “We have an agreement.” His brows lowered and his jaw tightened . “No. I mean, yeah, I know. But, I need to go .” She looked at the floor, cheeks heating, and if she had to explain human bodily functions to a damned bot she’d lose it . “Where do you think you’ll get to ?” “God dammit, I have to take a piss !” Ronin stared, blinking once . “If I have to spell it out for you any more clearly — ” She snapped her mouth shut when he raised a hand and pointed into the hallway . “What’s down there ?” “ Another room .” “So you expect me to go in the corner, or, what? Out the window?” Her embarrassment was rapidly shifting to irritation . “Follow,” he said, and despite her annoyance, she did . He only took a few steps, stopping before the first door to the right. She stared at him, arms crossed. Frowning, he opened the door, turned on the light, and moved aside . Lara leaned forward and looked into the room . It was much different from the others she’d seen. For one, it was far smaller, and the floor was tiled. There was a large white tub along the far wall, and a wash basin beneath a mirror. She’d seen people use such things back home, usually as planters for their meager crop allotments. Between the two was another porcelain object, about knee-high and bowl-shaped, that reminded her of the outhouses the humans used . They had things like it at Kitty’s, but Lara had never been comfortable enough to use them . “ So …” “I don’t have any personal experience, but I think you sit on the toilet,” Ronin said, and if there wasn’t dry amusement in his voice, she was crazy . “Don’t you dare make fun of me!” She glared at him and gestured at the porcelain seat. “You mean that thing ?” He nodded . Lara entered the room, shuddering at the chill of the tiles against her bare feet. Stopping in front of the toilet , she lifted the lid. The inside was halfway filled with water . “ In … this ?” “Yes. And you push down the handle when you’re done .” She tapped the silver handle on the box-shaped rear portion of the toilet. When it didn’t do anything, she pressed harder, and watched in startled amazement as the water inside the bowl swirled and vanished into the hole at the bottom. Then, on its own, the bowl refilled . “Did you see that?” She swung her gaze back to Ronin . “Maybe I’d best leave you to it,” he said. “If you turn the knobs in the tub, water will come out. Right for cold, left for hot. Clean yourself up. I’ll leave something dry for you to wear in the hallway .” He left, closing the door behind him, and Lara returned her attention to the toilet. After she examined it from top to bottom, she relieved herself. Her guilt in cleaning herself with some of the paper from the nearby roll was understandable; paper was so rare amongst humans that they’d never dream of using it in such a fashion. She watched the soiled water disappear with no less amazement than the first time . That done, she removed her wraps, untying the knots and dropping the wet cloths onto the floor. They fell with a heavy splat. She felt lighter without them, though no warmer. Her legs were caked with mud from the walk, and the tiles were smudged with her dirty footprints. She’d wash her legs and feet to avoid making any more of a mess and call it good . She stepped into the tub, running a hand over the strangely-textured curtain bunched to one side, and turned to examine the metal knobs on the wall. She’d rarely had the luxury of bathing; there was only one water pump for everyone to share, and Warlord often cut it off at his whim. It was far more important to keep people and crops watered than to be clean . Tilting her head, she turned the left knob. Water flowed from the spigot and pooled around her feet, immediately cloudy with grime. To her wonderment, it warmed. She cupped her hands and splashed her chilled body . Soon, the warmth became heat. It was quickly too much for her to handle. She turned the middle knob, hoping for some cool water to balance the flow. Water sprayed onto her from above, first ice cold, then scalding hot . Lara screamed and stumbled back. One of her flailing hands caught on the curtain, and she used it to pull herself over the edge of the tub. Her feet slipped the instant they touched the floor. She landed hard on her backside with another cry, legs sprawled . “Fuck!” Pain radiated from her ass and up her side . The door burst open and Ronin was suddenly in the room . Lara looked up at him with rounded eyes. There was a small gun in his right hand, its dull black metal absorbing the overhead light. He held a waded-up piece of cloth in his other hand. Their eyes met . “Are you all—” His gaze dipped . “Get out!” Lara screamed, crossing her arms over her chest pressing her legs together, knees raised . As though the situation were routine, he shifted his attention to the tub. Steam gathered at the ceiling. Random drops of water landed on Lara’s back. He placed the cloth — a shirt, she realized — next to the wash basin and stepped over her, reaching up to draw the curtain closed . She watched, paralyzed by anger, embarrassment, and fear, as he held a hand in the water for several seconds. Then he reached in and turned one of the knobs . “Should be fine now. Soap is in the corner.” He didn’t look at her as he spoke, and seemed to make a point of fixing his gaze on the hallway wall as he exited . Lara sat on the floor a long while after the door was closed before she finally uncurled from her position. He’d taken but a single look at her bare-ass body, adjusted the water, and left. Not only had he upheld his promise not to touch her, but he hadn’t even leered at her in her most vulnerable position . She placed a hand on the side of the tub, using it for support, and stood up. Groaning, she rubbed at the ache in her backside . Mimicking Ronin, she stuck her arm through the curtain to check the water. It was pleasantly warm now. She stepped carefully back into the tub and entered the water streaming from the spigot overhead. Blissful heat surrounded her, and she let herself melt in its embrace. Eventually, she found the soap he’d mentioned, grabbing it from the little rack in the corner . She’d clean more than her legs, after all . * * * R onin lingered in the hallway. The sound of the shower changed subtly, and he knew it was now caressing her naked body . He forced himself to walk toward his room. Forced himself to think about how strange running water sounded, after all his time in the Dust; plumbing was out of place in this world, just like functioning electric and undamaged buildings . Closing the door behind him, he struggled to maintain the chain of thoughts. Instead, the image of Lara rose to the forefront of his mind — her bare skin moist and pink from the hot water, her breasts firm, nipples erect. Though her ribs were visible at her sides, her narrow waist led to flaring hips and long, lithe legs. The legs of a dancer. A tantalizing patch of red hair beckoned his gaze lower … That single second before she’d covered herself and Ronin looked away would be emblazoned in his memory until he was disassembled or a second Black Out tore it away . One glimpse of Lara awoke more in Ronin than anything he’d seen at Kitty’s . Were it in his power, he’d dump the memory. It would’ve been more respectful to her, and having an image of her naked form to peruse at will wasn’t going to help him fulfill his agreement . No touching . He removed his gear, laying it out piece-by-piece atop the chest at the foot of the bed. First the pistol in his hand. Next, its spare magazine, holding the last six rounds of ammunition. Then a knife with a serrated edge, various hand tools, and a lighter that was almost empty. His belt followed, dust caked on the rugged material of its many pouches . Ronin stared at the objects, reaching down and adjusting their lay to achieve something close to symmetry. True balance was impossible with this eclectic array, but it all seemed right together, like the mismatched pieces formed some greater, cohesive whole . His thought-chains took strange, whimsical twists from there . Sometimes, out in the wastes, he’d uncover items that couldn’t possibly have any practical use, and he knew they’d been created by some human, long ago. Had the Creators shaped humans first? Were they the flawed children who inspired the eventual crafting of bots, a more perfect reflection of the Creators themselves? Despite the similarities between bots and humans, the differences were so stark, so undeniable . Humans were fragile, irrational, nonsensical creatures with more weaknesses than strengths. And Lara was one of them . Why had a human so suddenly caught his interest? All Ronin had craved since waking was knowledge of his purpose; he’d never discover the truth while he obsessed over a human woman . He undressed, blocking the onslaught of simulations that would approximate Lara removing her clothes. Such things weren’t real, no matter how they appeared. She was much more than data in his memory. Lara was a living, breathing creature. Everything she said and did would always belong to her . What failed paths of logic had brought him to this point, hiding in a room he rarely used to avoid the temptation of a woman he’d brought into his home ? She would dance for him, and he’d provide for her needs, nothing more. He’d watch her. Over time, he would discern why he’d been so enthralled by the way she moved. Then, foolish whim fulfilled, he would part ways with her. He’d turn his remaining credits into supplies he needed for the Dust, give Lara the remaining chits, and leave Cheyenne far behind . His purpose wasn’t in this place, wasn’t with her. The key to his programming lay somewhere in the ruins of the world. It was somewhere in the Dust, waiting for him, and he had to seek it out . But didn’t Ronin deserve a respite, after so many days in the wastes? Didn’t he deserve this one little diversion ?
